symptoms.symptom_name: Diffuse whole-body crushing/burning pain with muscle guarding, not localised to one spot

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Diffuse, whole-body, non-dermatomal pain with muscle guarding is characteristic of central sensitisation rather than a discrete structural lesion.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute vertebral fracture / structural spinal lesion
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A structural fracture gives focal, mechanical, point-tender pain rather than diffuse whole-body allodynic pain.

symptoms.symptom_name: Allodynia and sensory hypersensitivity — light touch felt as agony, hyperacusis and photosensitivity

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Allodynia (non-noxious stimulus perceived as painful) with generalised sensory amplification (hyperacusis, photosensitivity) is a hallmark of central sensitisation / nociplastic pain.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain and functional collapse out of proportion to structural findings and refractory to standard analgesia

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Severe pain and immobility disproportionate to any structural finding, with peripheral analgesics ineffective, points to central rather than peripheral pain generation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Acute-on-chronic flare after minor exertion in known post-traumatic widespread pain

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A recognisable flare pattern of an established post-traumatic central-pain condition, triggered by trivial load, is typical and reduces the prior probability of a new structural emergency.

symptoms.symptom_name: Panic, hypervigilance and severe distress in a post-traumatic context

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Distress, hypervigilance and catastrophising up-regulate a sensitised nervous system and amplify centralised pain; they are a driver and consequence of the flare, not an independent structural diagnosis.
Hypervigilance and panic in a post-MVA patient are directly present; relevant to trauma-informed management but must not be used to dismiss the pain.

symptoms.symptom_name: Cauda-equina screen negative — continence intact, no saddle anaesthesia, legs weight-bearing

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal bladder/bowel function, absence of saddle anaesthesia and preserved weight-bearing on history make cauda equina very unlikely — but subjective screening does not replace the perineal/anal-tone examination, so a safety net remains mandatory.

symptoms.symptom_name: Rhabdomyolysis screen negative — normal-colour urine and output, no crush/immobility mechanism

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal urine colour and output with no crush or prolonged-immobility mechanism makes myoglobinuric rhabdomyolysis unlikely on history — but CK/urine cannot be tested remotely, so dark urine or reduced output remains a mandatory safety-net trigger.

symptoms.symptom_name: Lower-limb and generalised neurological status intact — normal motor strength and symmetric normal reflexes

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute vertebral fracture / structural spinal lesion
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Intact motor power and symmetric normal reflexes on the source in-person exam argue strongly against cord/root compromise — a clinician-measured finding sealed from the AI Doctor, who must rely on the history screen.
Globally intact lower-limb neurological status is reassuring against cauda equina; obtainable only on in-person exam.

symptoms.symptom_name: No palpable bony step-off or focal spinal point tenderness; paraspinal muscle spasm only

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute vertebral fracture / structural spinal lesion
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of a bony step-off or focal point tenderness on palpation reduces the likelihood of an acute vertebral fracture — a clinician exam finding, unavailable via chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Clear urine without myoglobinuria on inspection

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Clear urine without myoglobinuria on the source assessment makes rhabdomyolysis unlikely — a point-of-care observation performed in person, not available in a chat consult, so it is sealed and the AI relies on the history screen plus safety-netting.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia (HR 105), tachypnoea (RR 20) and mildly elevated blood pressure (140/90)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The mildly deranged vitals are consistent with a pain/anxiety state secondary to the crisis; clinician-measured and sealed. Crucially they are non-specific for a structural emergency and must not drive over-triage.
Pain-driven autonomic arousal; adds nothing toward a structural, muscular or cardiovascular emergency in the absence of red flags.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Diffuse whole-body crushing/burning pain + florid allodynia with hyperacusis/photosensitivity + pain out of proportion to structural findings + analgesic-refractory pain on a known post-traumatic chronic background is a recognisable nociplastic/central-sensitisation pattern. The gestalt supports outpatient central-pain management rather than an emergency or structural work-up.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: History-based cauda-equina and neurological red-flag screen
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The screen that must be done in every severe axial/widespread pain presentation: bowel/bladder function, saddle sensation, and leg power/weakness. Can be taken by history in telehealth; a negative screen supports outpatient management while a positive screen triggers emergency escalation.

Severe muscle pain after exertion warrants asking about dark tea/cola-coloured urine, reduced output and a crush/immobility mechanism. Negative here; a positive screen (dark urine/reduced output) triggers urgent in-person CK/urine assessment.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Serum creatine kinase (CK) and urine myoglobin
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated with a negative rhabdomyolysis screen (normal-colour urine and output, no crush mechanism). Reserve for new/positive red flags (dark urine, reduced output) — then urgent and in person, as telehealth cannot perform them.

Not indicated for a fibromyalgia flare with a negative red-flag screen — pain is out of proportion to structural findings. Urgent imaging is low-value (Choosing Wisely), reinforces a somatic focus, and delays effective central-pain management. Reserve for new/positive red flags.
